title: Tamy Ogasawara -Team Interview | Client Operations Engineer (Growth Expert)
meeting_title: Tamy Ogasawara -Team Interview | Client Operations Engineer (Growth Expert)

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hello.
Hi.
How's it going?
Oh, good.
How about you?
Oh, really well.
Thank you.
Awesome.
Um, taking a little bit of a long weekend and so trying to get passed and so that's really nice.
Um, yeah.
Uh, well, thank you for joining today.
Uh, I obviously went through your resume and stuff and looked at your background and very cool background.
Um, I just want to kind want to get an idea of like, how did you get in?
Interested in doing more of like the building versus, you know, the products.
Because I went from engineering to product.
Oh, yeah, that's different.
Yeah, so I was an engineer and then I got into machine learning and data science through that.
And then I, you know, did DevRel for a while and then went into product.
And so, yeah, I'm just curious about kind of the opposite flow.
Awesome, perfect.
So a little intro about me.
I've been working for the past 10 years with digital in general.
So I started off my career with growth marketing, especially SEO and conversion rates optimization as well.
I worked as a digital marketer for about five to six years.
So I went like the career ladder, got into a management position for this.
And then I got interest more on the software.
heard the
So I did a migration to work as a product manager, which is what you just mentioned.
So I worked as a product manager for about four years of my career, especially on startups.
So I worked with startups in different stages, early stage, like 12 people type of startups, but also like Series B and Series C type of startups as well with like 100 plus employees.
So I think I got like a full cycle of like product market fit and then like scaling and going maybe to different products and different verticals.
So now for the past two years-ish, I've been working more with building stuff myself.
So automation, especially prompt engineering, and also working with Vibe coding in general.
Going to your question specifically.
Right, so what made me think that going to a builder type of role would make sense for me?
So this started off when I was working actually with SEO.
I started to be curious of how I could actually work with JavaScript, CSS, and HTML.
And the reason why is because I'm very curious about to know how things work.
So I'm not this type of person that will work, for example, with a broad idea of how a certain thing works.
Like, oh, SEO, HTML is important.
And we try to put some keywords and that's it.
So back in the days, I was really curious to know how the Google algorithm works.
Like, how it does a prioritization of X page versus Y page and this type of stuff.
So I got into the more technical side of things back in 2021-ish, 2020.
So I started to study JavaScript by myself, trying to learn, trying to understand.
And then a bit more.
Then as a natural step, I went to product because it was more involved into actually building something rather than just like working with something that already exists.
And as I was working with product, I also went for a full cycle.
So I started with growth, product management.
And then I went all the way for like security, product management.
I worked with the SRE team of one of the startups that I worked for.
And for me, was always like chasing my curiosity.
So how things work like behind the scenes, like how can I make sure that a website is secure?
And what is the business value of having a website with a high uptime?
What is the difference between 99% and 99.9% in terms of like business results?
So very curious person in general.
And I believe that
This curiosity led me to finally thinking, okay, how can I actually build something?
A bit of curiosity and a bit of like this natural feeling of I want to see things happening.
So naturally on bigger teams and on projects, one thing that used to happen is I wanted to prioritize and I didn't know exactly if the effort was high, low, or medium.
So I started to kind of like get more into building and get a bit anxious of I want to see results.
I am a very like results driven person.
So I started to like risk building one thing or two.
And then I saw that I liked it and I saw that things would happen faster and I would have the full like context of this is the business value of building this.
So this was a kind of like a natural step and I grew to like it essentially.
Yeah.
Nice.
That's awesome.
That's a super fun path.
I think, I think.
I actually like that's a better path in a way because like you're already a subject matter expert and then you can like then you can execute, you know, like you've got all that knowledge.
Yeah, so I think that's really cool.
Thanks for giving that background.
Yeah, kind of diving into some questions I have that are, you know, related to how we operate on the client ops team.
So a lot of times we're, I have, sorry, my dog, I gave her a new ball and it squeaks.
So if you hear that, like, that's what that is.
Oh, it's so good.
So, yeah, so a lot of times we're handed pretty vague requirements.
So how, tell me about a time where you had to like really kind of clarify the scope, understand the requirements and the acceptance criteria and all that good stuff.
This is an awesome question because on the past two years, I've worked as a contractor and a lot of the projects that I worked on, I had to refine them directly with the CEO, for example.
And the CEO won't like break down into like tasks and like definition of ready, definition of done, yada, yada.
So usually when I receive a project, and I'm very blessed that I worked as a product manager in the past, so it gives me a lot of background for that.
I start off by essentially understanding what is the definition of success of that particular project.
So kind of like as a tree breaking down, like this is the definition of success.
If we have a metric, great.
If we don't have a metric, at least a vision of what is success for the particular project.
And then I start to break this down either through like...

Interviewing a stakeholder, aka talking to them and making some questions, asking some whys and asking like how they see that like it would be a successful project in terms of like timeline, resources, cost.
Sometimes if it is a more technical person, if they already have a stack that they are interested in using and this type of stuff.
So then I can break into like success looks like this, break down into like having more characteristics of timeline stack, da, da, da, da, da, da.

And then when I have these already in a good shape, then usually if it is a project that is very, very vague, I try to propose like a POC or something along those lines of, okay, let's focus on a micro validation of if that's actually what you need.
And then for this POC, I break it down into like an epic, tasks, stories.
And then when I have all of these, like in a place that we have one, a micro objective to pursue, then I do the POC, which like in a broader sense, it could be like a prototype in general.
And then having the prototype going, then we have more clarity of what is actually required for the bigger like objective.
And then we can like go back and divide that into steps and try to pursue that particular metric.
Usually, if we can also have any type of measurement, KPI on the POC, it's very good if we can do a test or something.
If not, at least we have clarity of what would be the actual effort instead of trying to estimate efforts and like requirements without having a clear understanding of what is going on.
So I'd say that's the 80-20, yeah.
Yeah, yeah, no, that makes sense.
And you kind of answered this, but...
So since we are working directly with our internal, you know, customers, so to speak, the delivery team, the content, you know, folks, sometimes they don't actually know what success looks like.
They might have this, because I've talked to several of them where they're like, I don't actually have a rubric of, like, what makes an article good.
I just know it when I see it, and I know when I don't see it.

So, yeah, it sounds like that lightweight POC is kind of, would be, you know, your answer about, like, what about, what if this stakeholder doesn't actually know, like, exactly what they're looking for.
So I really like that, you know, you've dealt with some, you know, some of that ambiguity before, because that's, that is a lot of what we, we do building kind of those, those POCs.
And then iterating, you know, on that.
Yeah.

So, like, about, you know, I mean, especially, like, contracting, I don't know if you're faced with this more or less than you were as a, you know, employee, but how do you handle, like, when you get unrealistic expectations?
Like, you know, and unrealistic expectations could look anything from, hey, build this for me in two days, and I'm not going to give you any information about what it is.

Or it could be that, like, hey, this is, like, this insanely detailed, you know, like, spec doc from someone that doesn't really even understand how, you know, something's built, and then they want it, like, in a short amount of time or, you know, with just a few resources.
How do you kind of manage those unrealistic expectations?
Yeah, I believe that going back to the first answer that I gave, usually when we refine together, we are able to actually see what actually matters.
So one thing that I do a lot when I see that an expectation is unrealistic, I try to understand what exactly they want out of that particular project scope, etc.

So this is the first thing that I do, because usually we are able to like kill like 30%, 40% of the requirements, because we find out that the whole objective is to have, I don't know, an article generated.
And then we see that they actually liked a particular platform because they had a beautiful UI, but like beautiful UI is not the article, so we can focus on the article first.
So asking a lot of like, okay, but what is the exact purpose and what would be the thing that would bring you the most value?
Which would
also goes hand in hand with like, if they don't know what success looks like, what is the pain point that they are trying to mitigate, maybe.
So, for example, if it is like a CSS person or something like that, they spend a lot of time answering comments ABC.
And then we find out that they want to, I don't know, an agent that will answer comments.
And they have all these expectations about these agents.
But then we find out that actually they spend most of their time with like 70%.
It's always like the same five questions.
And then I can like say, okay, let's compromise with having the 70% already done because this will already save you a lot of time.

And usually when I talk to them in a way that I can see that they can see the impact of having just the essence, the core aspect of it, it's, it's easy to actually make them kind of like lower their expectation because actually their expectation, the core of
It is X.
So a lot of Ys, a lot of what is the pain points, a lot of like, what is the actual core value that you are trying to face?
So this is one thing.
Then another thing that we can always negotiate is timeline versus feature.
So one thing that I can also say to them is like, we can have either timeline or feature.
What do you prefer?
I want this feature that will be a crazy personalized thing.
We are able to do it, but then the timeline will have to change a little bit.
Or we'll have to have like external resources or another engineer working together with me so we can like do parallel work, etc.

So I always try to like break down into like resources, timeline, value, so we can like at least pull one of these, which is like easier than pulling all of them, like making a longer timeline to deliver less and with more resources, for example.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So I'd say that this is also one thing that I try to like break down together with a person.

And last but not least, if we truly need something, we cannot like change the timeline, we cannot have more resources than trying to find something open source or something that a person already did like in the past or a lower cost type of thing that already gets us like 50% the way there costing like 200 bucks.
Then it's also something that I try to do if it is really something that needs to be done on a particular timeline that is like very challenging.
Yeah, no, that makes a ton of sense.
I think what we do is a lot lighter weight, like, you know, those are all things that I've done in the past as a product person too, you know.
Here it is different simply because like we are working.
Working with our internal writers and, you know, they're slammed and so you don't always have like all of, you know, the information.
They don't have time, you know, to like really give you much feedback or there's a lot of shoulder tapping.
So I will say that it's like I haven't put on my like product manager hat here very often just because it is very scrappy and stuff.
So it's kind of having to make that mental switch of like, okay, it's faster to just build this and ask questions later because it's taking a week to get any feedback from them.
So, yeah, it's also a challenge.
Yeah, yeah.
So it is different and it definitely required for me, like, you know, making a mental switch, I'll say, from more traditional product work.
Um, yeah, so, uh, I think, yeah, you answered a lot of my questions about kind of balancing speed, cost, and quality.
Um, when it comes to, you know, you have a lot more understanding and background than I do about this because I've typically worked in MLOps, um, for most of my career.
And so the features that we build are very different.
We're not generating content.
And so, um, like, when, like, what do you use to measure quality, uh, for generating content and, you know, how do you check, um, for things like drift in the, the quality over time?
Um, so quality for contents.
There are many metrics that we can measure.
Some are more objective.
Um, so, um, uh, uh

So, for example, if the content has an SEO purpose, then there are some things that we can do, for example, doing a research of what are the articles that are ranking on the top positions right now, and trying to find, for example, what are the terms that they use, what is like the keyword density, and what are, how many like H2s they have, what is the H1, meta title, meta description.
We can also do something a bit more advanced in terms of trying to map intent with FAQ, like frequently asked questions, etc.

So, these are all things that could be very easily measured in terms of, let's take the top 10, let's see the average of what they are doing in terms of like numbers, and let's try to do something similar.
So, this is kind of like the first thing that I would do to measure quality.
Then, second thing that I do to measure quality is essentially...
Especially engagement, this can also be a way that we can measure more like, quote unquote, subjective type of quality.

So if we are able to essentially do a test where we have like a piece of content live for like an audience, and we can see how long they are actually like spending time to read it, and we can actually have a heat map, for example, of if they stopped on a particular section to take a look into it for a longer period of time, this is also interesting to have.
So I'll likely say if we are able to test engagement, it would be awesome.
On the more operational side of things, for example, I have a content ops person, and they are generating the articles with our tool.
One thing that I can see is how many times they regenerated it.
I can also ask for feedback, for example, on particular sections.
So if they want to regenerate just one section.
Before they regenerate, collect feedback of why they are regenerating.
So definitely from the content ops side of things is one thing that we can definitely monitor.

And then at the other end, on a more, I would say, lagging type of KPI, if we have a relationship with the clients and we know the life cycle of that particular content, I can see if that content is ranking, receiving impressions, receiving clicks, average position on Google, this type of more like lagging that would take, I don't know, three months for me to actually have this data.

But if I have a longer relationship with the client, then I'm able to also check this type of metric as well, which is essentially the business result that we want to deliver is the content to rank at the end of the day.
So definitely would be one thing that is interesting to have.
That's awesome.
Yeah.
No, that's super, super cool.

As I said, I don't have, like, obviously I've been here for about four or five months, five months probably, so I've been learning, you know, how people evaluate the quality of content, and it is very interesting.
I never really thought of it in those terms, but I've also worked, besides MLAPS, I've worked in data labeling, so it's not so dissimilar.
For sure, for sure, there are a lot of similarities, actually.
Yeah, yeah.
Cool, so I want to give you time to ask me some questions.
Awesome.
One thing that I love to know is, like, how is your day-to-day working at GrowthX?
Like, what are the main things that you do and main challenges that you tackle on a daily basis?
Yeah, so my role is a little bit different.
Um, then say Kirkland's, uh, because I, I kind of run the cycles or sprints, however you want to, you know, whatever terminology you want to use.
And so, and I haven't really done that kind of project management before.
So that was a learning curve for me.
I've done plenty of product work, but I've typically relied on engineering leads or program managers to do that type of work.
So that's, um, that's definitely been a learning curve for me.
Um, just, you know, really being, you know, managing it at a more granular level, like at the ticket level.

And so my day to day is really just checking, like triaging new tickets that come in from the, you know, um, content team, understanding, you know, if anything needs to be put in the current cycle, um, urgent, you know, items.
And so doing a lot of that, you know, it's.

Tapping on the shoulders, doing a lot of that, managing kind of the special projects of rolling out, you know, like Daniel built agentic workflows to replace some older pipelines that we had that are generating content.
And so I led both the projects, but also the execution of a couple of different ones.
Different ones.
And so that's been interesting doing, you know, more prompt engineering, which I had done, like, I took a sabbatical a while before this job.
And I did some, like, built some multi-agentic workflows with crew AI.
And so I'd done some prompt engineering and stuff.
But yeah, it's, it's very, it's a very different type of building.
Yeah.
Mm-hmm.
Mm Mm
So, again, kind of doing a lot of that as part of my day-to-day and the other engineers do that type of work as well.
And so it's, as I said, like mine's a little bit more mixed.
Sometimes I help with more technical customers.
I really hope you can't hear that.
Zoom's pretty good.
Like she's squeaking her ball really well.
It's ear-piercing for me.
This is my bad choice.

But, yeah, so helping the content team, like edit and review, like super technical content, like, you know, multi-GPU deployments, KB Cash, you know, just stuff that's more on the MLOP side and that's more technical.
That's cool.
That's That's
So kind of doing that, but also kind of refining the pipelines to make sure that we can generate technical content like that and that it is accurate.
So, yeah, it's a little bit of everything.
And then sometimes, like, I'm trying to make the time to ramp up more on building workflows.

I've done a couple, like, little side projects and stuff, but I haven't, like, that's something that I think, like, Kirkland, Marko, and Nico do more of because I'm spending a lot of time doing the project management side of things.
And I work closer with the delivery team, the content team, I would say, a little bit as far as, like, more of a bigger picture, you know, management sort of thing.
But, yeah, so that's kind of more my day-to-day.
I would would

Say, like, the regular engineers on the team, their day-to-day is more on just grabbing a ticket that looks, you know, kind of interesting or they have a background in, you know, say it's like programmatic SEO and, you know, they create a workflow with some customization, for instance.
The workflows, generally speaking, like, we have a lot of, you know, just pre-built workflows that people have built in the past.
So it's a lot of reusing and kind of tweaking to suit the customer's need.
So they tend to do a lot of that.
And that's more of their day-to-day.
They're able to, like, you know, really focus a lot more on, you know, doing that type of work than I am.
Awesome.
Awesome.
I think another question that I have is, what makes you excited?
Are for the next six months?
Is there a particular mission, vision, et cetera, that makes you excited about working at growthx?
I'm really excited to finish up rolling over some old pipelines to a GenTech workload.
I think we'll finish that up in this cycle because I've done the bulk of the work on that and it's very repetitive and it's not intellectually stimulating whatsoever.
But it's, you know, sometimes I'll pick up that type of work to free the engineers to, you know, focus on things that are coming, you know, like fresh out of the belt.
So I'm excited to, you know, finish up those projects and get back to, you know, doing some building and, you know, possibly product work.
So far, I haven't done any product work here, but...
product excited.
now, I'm
Um, there might be opportunity to, to take over some of the projects that have, you know, already been started.
And because right now, like Daniel does all the product management for all the, you know, different product lines and features and, and teams.
And so there might be some opportunity to do that, to do that.
Um, but I think I'm most, you know, excited about getting back to coding.
Like, um, I had the most fun doing that, you know, during my sabbatical.
And then also, uh, just the little bit I've, I've been able to squeeze in here, you know, in the evenings, just trying to do like some learning.
Uh, so that's what I'm most excited about.
So it's not like a, you know, we have so many different initiatives.
Like there's so many different opportunities that it can be a little bit overwhelming, I think, to, to really decide like where you want to.
You know, being the most useful, but I think with my background, there is.
You know, there are some clear areas where I think, you know, the MLOps background and data labeling and stuff would be useful in a product sense, but hopefully also in a, you know, builder sense.

So, yeah, and it is like, it is one of those companies where you can, I wouldn't say completely choose your own adventure, but there's a lot of flexibility in being able to, you know, kind of take control of your destiny.

So far, I haven't had time to do that, but the opportunity is there now, you know, as I said, especially since like some of these projects are, you know, ending, I'll be able to actually think and pay attention more on what else is going on in the company and kind of choose where I want to be.
So that's what I'm most excited in general about is kind of being able to choose my own.
Yeah, based on what I talked with Kirkland, I can see that definitely is a company that has a lot of challenges and like people that are like intellectually curious to like explore new challenges.
So this is something that excites me as well.
I can see that there are a lot of smart people over there.
And this is kind of like my main motivation of wanting to to like hop on like a bigger team and stuff is to learn a lot and explore a lot.

And this is definitely something that seems to be a point in common that you two say like there are a lot of opportunities over here and people want to do a lot of stuff and sometimes even overwhelming like the amount of opportunities.
yeah, definitely.
Yeah.
So I think we're at time.
But if you have some more questions, I have a little time after this.
I think these are my main questions.
Maybe one, like if you have two more, one to two more minutes is
What do you think that are kind of the main characteristics on people that are successful in the growthx?
So it's like curious, like self-learning, or like, I don't know, entrepreneurial, maybe, I don't know.
Actually, I would say none of those things.
I would say, and that's probably a different answer than you would get from other people, but I would say paying attention to quality, that is something that we sometimes struggle with.
And I think it's important for whether it's an engineer or a content manager, you know, really taking ownership and responsibility for the work that they're doing.
And, you know, like, yes, we want to move fast.
And sometimes I...
Struggle and, like, over-rotate on quality versus speed and, you know, and then I'll correct myself and then, you know, maybe over-rotate the other way.
So it's hard to find the balance, I think, here between, and I've never struggled with that.

And so it's a unique, I think it's a unique problem that we have because quality of content is so subjective and, you know, really trying to understand, you know, what it means to the customer and, you know, what they say it means might not actually be what, you know, it's to them.

And so, yeah, I think that's the most difficult thing is, like, still being able to move really fast while maintaining a high bar of, you know, quality and making sure that, you know, everyone's taking ownership of their work.
Awesome.
Perfect.
Thank you.
Thank you.
I think you gave me a pretty clear picture of like your experience and how things are at GrowthX.
So thank you for that.
Yeah, absolutely.
If you think of anything else that you're curious about, just shoot Tucker an email and he'll get it to me.
Yeah.
Or if you have my email, you know, from this calendar invite, you can directly email to me too.
Thank you.
Thank you.
appreciate that.
It was a pleasure meeting you.
Thank you.
You too.
You have a great day.
You too.
Cheers.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Tamy Ogasawara for the Client Operations Engineer role.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=38.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Candidate Profile:** Tamy's career path—Growth Marketing → Product Management → Engineering—provides a unique perspective, combining business strategy with technical execution.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=92.0)
  - [**Ambiguity Management:** Tamy uses a structured process to clarify vague requirements: define success metrics, interview stakeholders to find the core problem, and build a lightweight POC to validate the approach.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=403.0)
  - [**Expectation Management:** To handle unrealistic requests, Tamy focuses on the core value, negotiates the timeline vs. feature scope, and proposes using open-source tools to accelerate delivery.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=666.0)
  - [**Role Alignment:** The role requires balancing speed with quality, a key challenge given the subjective nature of content and the need for high ownership. Tamy's proposed quality metrics (SEO analysis, engagement tracking, user feedback) directly address this.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1048.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background & Motivation

  - [**Career Path:** 10 years in digital, moving from Growth Marketing (SEO/CRO) to Product Management, and now to hands-on building (automation, prompt engineering).](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=92.0)
  - [**Motivation:** Driven by a deep curiosity ("how things work") and a desire for faster, more tangible results than larger teams often allow.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=177.0)
  - [**Product Management Experience:** Worked at startups (12-person to Series C) on full-cycle product development, from security to SRE.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=121.0)

### Handling Vague Requirements & Unrealistic Expectations

  - [**Process for Vague Requirements:**](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=403.0)
    1.  [**Define Success:** Establish clear success metrics or a vision.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=456.0)
    2.  [**Interview Stakeholders:** Ask "why" to uncover core problems, timelines, and tech preferences.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=476.0)
    3.  [**Propose a POC:** Build a lightweight prototype to validate the approach and clarify requirements.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=513.0)
  - [**Process for Unrealistic Expectations:**](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=666.0)
    1.  [**Find Core Value:** Ask "what is the exact purpose?" to identify and remove non-essential requirements (often 30–40%).](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=721.0)
    2.  [**Negotiate Timeline vs. Feature:** Offer a clear trade-off: more features require more time or resources.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=851.0)
    3.  [**Leverage Open Source:** Use existing tools to accelerate delivery for tight deadlines.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=906.0)

### Measuring Content Quality

  - [**Challenge:** Content quality is subjective, requiring a multi-faceted approach.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1048.0)
  - [**Proposed Metrics:**](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1051.0)
      - [**Objective SEO Analysis:** Analyze top-ranking articles for keyword density, H-tag structure, and meta descriptions to create a measurable benchmark.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1080.0)
      - [**User Engagement:** Track time-on-page and heatmaps to measure user interest.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1133.0)
      - [**Operational Feedback:** Monitor user regeneration counts and collect feedback on *why* a section was regenerated.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1178.0)
      - [**Lagging Business KPIs:** Track long-term metrics like Google rankings, impressions, and clicks to measure business impact.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1210.0)

### GrowthX Team & Culture

  - [**Stevie's Role:** Manages sprints, triages tickets, leads special projects (e.g., migrating to agentic workflows), and reviews technical content.](https://fathom.video/share/UbxtqXfqbPpeuTrNvnfZpbdAci9KL7as?tab=summary&timestamp=1304.0)
